1. To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A we need to find the probability that $X_B > X_A$. This is equivalent to finding the probability that $X_B - X_A > 0$. Since $X_A$ and $X_B$ are independent normal random variables the difference $X_B - X_A$ is also normally distributed with mean $\mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5$ and variance $\sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2 = 12^2 + 10^2 = 144 + 100 = 244$. The standard deviation of the difference is $\sqrt{244} \approx 15.62$. We can now calculate the probability using the standard normal distribution. The z-score is $(0 - 5) / 15.62 \approx -0.32$. The probability that $X_B > X_A$ is the probability that a standard normal variable is greater than -0.32 which is approximately 0.6255. Therefore the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A is approximately 0.6255.

2. To determine the time $t$ at which both groups have the same performance improvement level we need to solve the equation $P_A(t) = P_B(t)$. This means solving $50 e^{0.02t} = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}}$. This equation can be solved numerically or graphically. Using numerical methods we find that the solution is approximately $t \approx 10$. At this time both groups have the same performance improvement level. The explanation for this process involves understanding the mathematical models that describe the performance improvement of each group. The force of the models is that they provide a way to predict the performance improvement over time. Nothing is gained by ignoring the differences in the models. The process of solving this equation requires careful application of mathematical techniques.
